name: Ḥasan, attendant of Mullá Ḥusayn
summary: The personal attendant of Mullá Ḥusayn, arrested and brutally tortured (his nose pierced and a cord passed through it) and paraded through the streets in reprisal against his master.
importance_reason: Incidental episode participant known only for the cruelty inflicted on him; three mentions, single source.

name: Ḥasan-i-S̱ha‘r-Báf-i-Yazdí
summary: A Bábí of Yazd (a silk-weaver by trade) named in passing in the Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: One-clause incidental name with no narrative beyond the bare mention.

name: Ḥasan-‘Alí Ḵhán-i-Karrúsí
summary: A Sunní tribal chief of Karrús appointed by the Amír-Niẓám to reinforce the siege of Zanján; he commanded two regiments and took the fort of ‘Alí-Mardán Khán (later, per Nabíl, Persian minister to Paris).
importance_reason: Named military commander in the Zanján upheaval with a recurring role, but a secondary, replaceable officer.

name: Ḥusayn
summary: One of the ‘ulamás of Káshán who abetted the Amír-Niẓám's orders against the Seven Martyrs of Ṭihrán.
importance_reason: A passing co-actor in a major event, named only as 'a certain Ḥusayn'; single mention.

name: Ḥusayn (seven-year-old son of Ḥujjat)
summary: The seven-year-old son of Ḥujjat-i-Zanjání, coaxed by the governor into revealing a secret during the Zanján upheaval.
importance_reason: Memorable single-anecdote child figure tied to a major hero, but only one incident and one mention.

name: Ḥusayn Khán-i-Ájúdán-Báshí
summary: Governor of Fárs (1844–1848) and a Qájár military commander/diplomat; the first official to arise against the Báb, ordering His arrest and the cruel maltreatment of His early disciples (notably Mullá Ṣádiq-i-Muqaddas), and later stigmatized in scripture as a 'wine-bibber' and 'tyrant' who died in disgrace.
importance_reason: Foremost early persecutor of the Báb (35 mentions, both books, high rel-degree); a taught secondary antagonist of the Shíráz period.
characterizations: GPB: The villainous Ḥusayn Khán, the Niẓámu’d-Dawlih, the governor of Fárs; vindictive, relentless... the 'wicked and accursed' Ḥusayn Khán
GPB: Ḥusayn Khán, the governor of Shíráz, stigmatized as a 'wine-bibber' and a 'tyrant,' the first who arose to ill-treat the Báb
Wikipedia: Hossein Khan Ajudanbashi was a 19th-century Iranian military commander and diplomat who served as governor of Fars from 1844 to 1848 during the reign of Mohammad Shah Qajar.

name: Ḥusayn ibn-i-Rúḥ Naw-baḵhtí
summary: The third of the four 'gates' (abwáb) who, in Twelver Shí'í tradition, served as intermediary to the Hidden Imám during the Lesser Occultation; cited illustratively in the Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: A historical Islamic figure referenced only as a doctrinal analogy; single mention, not a Bábí-era actor.

name: Ḥusayn-i-Iṣfahání
summary: A Bábí of Iṣfahán, brother of Abu’l-Qásim, named among the believers of that city.
importance_reason: Minor named believer with two bare mentions and no developed story.

name: Ḥusayn-i-Mílán
summary: A siyyid of Mílán, bayoneted by soldiers as a Bábí martyr.
importance_reason: Single-mention martyr-list entry with one line of narrative.

name: Ḥusayn-i-Taftí
summary: An eighteen-year-old convert of Taft, denounced by his own father and torn to pieces before his mother's eyes during the early-20th-century persecutions in Yazd.
importance_reason: Single-mention martyr with a vivid but isolated anecdote.

name: Ḥusayn-i-‘Arab (grandfather of Nabíl)
summary: Forebear named only genealogically — the father of Ghulám-‘Alí and grandfather of the chronicler Nabíl-i-A‘ẓam.
importance_reason: Pure genealogical mention with no role of his own.

name: Ḥusayn-‘Alí Ḵhán-i-S̱háh-Sún
summary: Commander of the Sháh-Sún regiment dispatched to Tákur in the district of Núr, where Bahá’u’lláh's ancestral home stood.
importance_reason: Named officer with a minor recurring role tied to events in Núr; three mentions, single source.

name: Ḥusayn-‘Alíy-i-Iṣfahání
summary: A believer of Iṣfahán in whose house certain remains were deposited; named in passing.
importance_reason: Single-mention incidental believer identified only by the use of his house.

name: Ḥáfiẓ
summary: The renowned 14th-century Persian lyric poet of Shíráz, quoted in the Dawn-Breakers; not a Bábí-era figure.
importance_reason: A culturally major poet but only quoted illustratively; likely duplicate of the next entry.

name: Ḥáfiẓ-i-Shírází
summary: The 14th-century Persian poet of Shíráz, whose verses were quoted (in extremis) by a Bábí; almost certainly the same person as the preceding 'Ḥáfiẓ' entry.
importance_reason: Major Persian poet quoted in passing; flagged as a likely duplicate of 'Ḥáfiẓ'.

name: Ḥájí 'Abdu'l-Majíd-i-Níshápúrí
summary: A turquoise-mine owner of Níshápúr and survivor of the struggle of Ṭabarsí who flung away his satchel of turquoises to follow the Báb; father of Badí‘ (the Iqán's bearer), he was himself martyred in Khurásán in old age.
importance_reason: A taught Ṭabarsí hero and father of the celebrated Badí‘; 13 mentions across both books.
characterizations: GPB: that induced the father of Badí‘, one of that gallant company, to fling unhesitatingly by the roadside the satchel, full of turquoises which he had brought from his father’s mine in Níshápúr
GPB: the father of Badí‘, both survivors of the struggle of Ṭabarsí

name: Ḥájí 'Alí Khán-i-Marághi'í, Ḥájibu'd-Dawlih
summary: Qájár court official (Ḥájibu'd-Dawlih) and chief executioner (Farrásh-Báshí) who superintended the killing of the Seven Martyrs of Ṭihrán and the persecution of many Bábís; he later met a gruesome end at the hands of the Lurs, who forced him to eat his own beard and rode him bridled before the people.
importance_reason: Key secondary antagonist whose retributive death is a taught episode; 18 mentions across both books.
characterizations: GPB: that bloodthirsty fiend, who had strenuously hounded down so many innocent and defenseless Bábís, fell in his turn a victim to the fury of the turbulent Lurs, who... cut off his beard, and forced him to eat it, saddled and bridled him, and rode him before the eyes of the people.

name: Ḥájí Abu'l-Ḥasan-i-S̱hírází
summary: A fellow-traveller of the Báb during His pilgrimage to Ḥijáz who described the voyage and was profoundly transformed by witnessing the Báb's majesty on a memorable Friday.
importance_reason: Named eyewitness-narrator of the pilgrimage with a memorable transformation anecdote; two mentions, single source.

name: Ḥájí Abu’l-Qásim-i-Shírází
summary: A wealthy Persian Bahá'í convert from whom a corrupt consul-general extorted a hundred thousand túmáns, cited as evidence of official rapacity.
importance_reason: Single-mention believer known only as the victim of an extortion anecdote.

name: Ḥájí Abu’l-Ḥasan-i-Ardikání
summary: An eminent follower of Bahá’u’lláh (c. 1830s–1928) who served as Trustee of the Ḥuqúqu'lláh, was numbered among the Apostles of Bahá’u’lláh, and was posthumously named a Hand of the Cause; famed for his austere life and for once being able to see Bahá’u’lláh only in a public bath without any sign of recognition.
importance_reason: A Hand of the Cause and Apostle of Bahá’u’lláh, Trustee of Ḥuqúqu'lláh — a taught and venerated figure despite the single GPB mention.
characterizations: GPB: the self-denying Ḥájí Abu’l-Ḥasan-i-Ardikání, surnamed Amín-i-Iláhí (Trusted of God)... was only able to do so in a public bath, where it had been arranged that he should see Bahá’u’lláh without approaching Him or giving any sign of recognition

Wikipedia: Mullá Abu'l-Hasan-i-Ardikání (1831–1928)... served as the trustee of Huqúqu'lláh, was posthumously appointed a Hand of the Cause of God by Shoghi Effendi, and identified as one of the nineteen Apostles of Baháʼu'lláh.

name: Ḥájí Akbar (kad-ḵhudá of Rastáq)
summary: The village headman (kad-khudá) of Rastáq into whose hands a captive was delivered to be brought before Vaḥíd.
importance_reason: Single-mention local official named only for handling a captive.

name: Ḥájí Alláh-Vardí
summary: A man of Qazvín renowned for his spiritual insight, father of Muḥammad-Javád-i-Farhádí, consulted in the Dawn-Breakers narrative.
importance_reason: Minor named Qazvín figure with two mentions; possibly the same family as the next entries (flagged).

name: Ḥájí Alláh-Vardíy-i-Farhádí
summary: A pious man of Qazvín, brother of the martyr Ḥájí Asadu’lláh-i-Farhádí; likely identical to 'Ḥájí Alláh-Vardí' above.
importance_reason: Single-mention Farhádí-family member; flagged as a probable duplicate of 'Ḥájí Alláh-Vardí'.

name: Ḥájí Alláh-Yár
summary: A believer through whose help the bodies of martyrs were removed to the silk factory of a Bábí of Mílán after the martyrdom of the Báb in Tabríz.
importance_reason: Named agent in the recovery of the Báb's and companions' remains — a taught episode; three mentions across both books.

name: Ḥájí Asadu’lláh-i-Farhádí
summary: A noted merchant of Qazvín of high repute for piety, brother of Ḥájí Alláh-Vardí and uncle of the Farhádí brothers; martyred at midnight as a Bábí.
importance_reason: Single-mention Qazvín martyr with a brief but pointed account of his death.

name: Ḥájí Báqir-i-Káshání
summary: A Bahá'í who, with Siyyid Ḥusayn-i-Káshání, helped establish the first Bahá'í center in Egypt.
importance_reason: Single-mention believer credited with a minor pioneering role in Egypt.

name: Ḥájí G̱hulám
summary: A man into whose house captured Bábís were gathered and held pending instructions from Ṭihrán.
importance_reason: Single-mention figure named only for the use of his house as a holding place.

name: Ḥájí Hás̱him, surnamed ‘Aṭṭár
summary: A prominent merchant well versed in Islamic scripture who recounted an episode of Mullá ‘Alí being summoned before the government.
importance_reason: Single-mention eyewitness-narrator of one episode.

name: Ḥájí Ja‘far-i-Tabrízí
summary: A believer who, on finding his name omitted from the list of those permitted to accompany Bahá’u’lláh into exile, cut his throat with a razor but was prevented in time from dying — an emblematic act of devotion.
importance_reason: Single-mention believer but with a memorable, frequently-told anecdote of self-sacrificing devotion.

name: Ḥájí Kalbásí
summary: A late mujtahid of Iṣfahán, named genealogically as the father of Muḥammad-Mihdí (the Safíhu’l-‘Ulamá); likely the same person as Ḥájí Muḥammad-Ibráhím-i-Kalbásí.
importance_reason: Single bare mention; flagged as a probable duplicate of 'Ḥájí Muḥammad-Ibráhím-i-Kalbásí'.

name: Ḥájí Khán Núrí
summary: A man entrusted, with Mírzá ‘Abdu’lláh Navayy, with securing supplies during an episode in the Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: Single-mention logistical figure with no developed role.

name: Ḥájí Káẓim Giltúg̱hí
summary: A Bábí martyr blown to pieces from the mouths of mortars alongside Sulaymán the shoemaker.
importance_reason: Single-mention martyr-list entry with one line of narrative.

name: Ḥájí Mullá Ismá‘íl
summary: A Bábí martyr who, when his turn came to be beheaded, refused to recant, declaring that even for money none should touch him.
importance_reason: Single-mention martyr with a memorable defiant saying.

name: Ḥájí Mullá Ismá‘íl-i-Faráhání
summary: A believer named in a list of companions alongside Mírzá Muḥammad-Ḥusayn-i-Ḥakamíy-i-Kirmání and others.
importance_reason: Single-mention name appearing only in a roster of believers.

name: Ḥájí Mullá Ismá‘íl-i-Qumí
summary: An early believer who joined the companions proceeding to Badasht, where he received from Bahá’u’lláh the appellation Sirru’l-Vujúd ('the Mystery of Existence').
importance_reason: Participant at the pivotal Badasht conference who received a title from Bahá’u’lláh; four mentions, single source.

name: Ḥájí Mullá Maḥmúd, the Niẓámu’l-‘Ulamá
summary: Tutor of the crown prince Náṣiri'd-Dín Mírzá and president of the ecclesiastical convocation in Tabríz that examined the Báb; it was to his questions that the Báb gave His celebrated affirmations of His station.
importance_reason: Presided over the taught Tabríz examination of the Báb; 12 mentions across both books.
characterizations: GPB: Ḥájí Mullá Maḥmúd, the Niẓámu’l-‘Ulamá, the Prince’s tutor, under whose presidency the examination convocation was held

name: Ḥájí Mullá Mihdí
summary: A late cleric, author of the Muḥriqu’l-Qulúb on the martyrdom of the Imám Ḥusayn, and great-grandfather of the Bábí Ḥájí Mírzá Kamálu’d-Dín-i-Naráqí.
importance_reason: Single-mention figure cited only as an author and ancestor.

name: Ḥájí Mullá Muḥammad-i-Ḥamzih, the S̱harí‘at-Madár
summary: A cleric who buried the bodies of martyrs near the Masjid-i-Káẓim-Big and induced their murderer to repent.
importance_reason: Single-mention figure with a brief act of mercy; possibly related to 'Ḥájí Muḥammad-‘Alíy-i-Ḥamzih' (different given name — not merged).

name: Ḥájí Mullá Mírzá Muḥammad Andirmání
summary: A cleric ordered by the premier, with Ḥájí Mullá ‘Alí Kiní, to examine the beliefs of Ṭáhirih.
importance_reason: Single-mention cleric tied to the examination of Ṭáhirih.

name: Ḥájí Mullá Taqí, the Ḥujjatu’l-Islám
aliases: Mullá Taqí (uncle and father-in-law of Ṭáhirih)
summary: Leading mujtahid of Qazvín (d. 1847), uncle and father-in-law of Ṭáhirih, and the foremost early opponent of Shaykhism (the first to pronounce takfír against Shaykh Aḥmad); his assassination in his own mosque — blamed on the Bábís and on Ṭáhirih — triggered a wave of persecution against the Bábí community of Qazvín.
importance_reason: Pivotal antagonist whose murder is a major taught episode central to Ṭáhirih's story; 16 mentions.
characterizations: DB: ‘Ḥájí Mullá Taqí, the Ḥujjatu’l-Islám, has pronounced him a heretic and decreed his expulsion from the town.’
Wikipedia: Mohammad Taqi Baraghani (died 25 October 1847) was a prominent Shiʻa cleric in Qajar Iran... known for being the first cleric to declare takfir (c. 1822) against Shaykh Ahmad al-Ahsá'í.

name: Ḥájí Mullá Ḥasan-i-‘Ammú
summary: A learned and devout divine delegated by the assembled ‘ulamás of Baghdád to put questions to Bahá’u’lláh and to request a miracle as proof of His mission — an exchange that became a celebrated test of the Baghdád period.
importance_reason: Central figure in the taught 'demand for a miracle' episode at Baghdád; single GPB mention but doctrinally significant.
characterizations: GPB: the assembled divines delegated the learned and devout Ḥájí Mullá Ḥasan-i-'Ammú, recognized for his integrity and wisdom, to submit various questions to Bahá'u'lláh for elucidation

name: Ḥájí Mullá Ṣáliḥ-i-Baraqání
summary: A highly reputed mujtahid of Qazvín (c. 1753 – mid 19th c.) heading an eminent ecclesiastical family; father of Ṭáhirih (Fáṭimih Baraghání) and brother of Mullá Taqí.
importance_reason: Father of Ṭáhirih and head of the foremost Qazvín clerical family; 10 mentions across both books.
characterizations: GPB: A scion [Ṭáhirih] of the highly reputed family of Ḥájí Mullá Ṣáliḥ-i-Baraqání, whose members occupied an enviable position in the Persian ecclesiastical hierarchy.

name: Ḥájí Mullá ‘Abdu’l-Vahháb-i-Qazvíní
summary: A distinguished mujtahid of Qazvín who hosted Siyyid Muḥammad and became an enemy of the Baraqání family.
importance_reason: Recurring (four mentions) Qazvín cleric tied to the Baraqání feud, but a secondary figure; single source.

name: Ḥájí Mullá ‘Alí Kiní
summary: A cleric ordered, with Ḥájí Mullá Mírzá Muḥammad Andirmání, to examine Ṭáhirih's beliefs; he broke off the discussion calling her an 'infidel'. Likely the same person as 'Ḥájí Mullá ‘Alíy-i-Kaní' (next).
importance_reason: Single-mention examiner of Ṭáhirih; flagged as a probable duplicate of 'Ḥájí Mullá ‘Alíy-i-Kaní'.

name: Ḥájí Mullá ‘Alíy-i-Kaní
summary: A leading mujtahid of Ṭihrán (c. 1806–1888) and 'clerical firebrand' who, with Siyyid Ṣádiq-i-Ṭabáṭabá’í, agitated against the Faith; the most powerful cleric of Ṭihrán in his day.
importance_reason: A leading anti-Faith mujtahid of Ṭihrán; single GPB mention but historically prominent. Flagged as likely the same person as 'Ḥájí Mullá ‘Alí Kiní'.
characterizations: GPB: Nor were the clerical firebrands, Ḥájí Mullá ‘Alíy-i-Kaní and Siyyid Ṣádiq-i-Ṭabáṭabá’í, the two leading mujtahids of Ṭihrán... willing to allow the slightest opportunity to pass without striking

name: Ḥájí Muḥammad Ibráhím-i-Khalíl
summary: Recipient of a Tablet of Bahá’u’lláh that foretold one of His sons from whose tongue God would cause the signs of His power to stream forth.
importance_reason: Single-mention recipient of a noted Tablet; significance attaches to the Tablet, not the man.

name: Ḥájí Muḥammad Káẓim
summary: A man resembling Mírzá Yaḥyá (Ṣubḥ-i-Azal) whom the latter sent to obtain a passport under a false name.
importance_reason: Single-mention figure used only as a body-double in a Mírzá Yaḥyá anecdote.

name: Ḥájí Muḥammad Ḥusayn Khán-i-Iṣfahání
summary: An Iṣfahán notable named genealogically as a grandfather of another figure.
importance_reason: Purely genealogical mention with no role of his own.

name: Ḥájí Muḥammad-Bisáṭ
summary: A man well versed in Islamic metaphysics and an admirer of both Shaykh Aḥmad and Siyyid Káẓim.
importance_reason: Single-mention figure characterized only by his Shaykhí sympathies.

name: Ḥájí Muḥammad-Ibráhím-i-Kalbásí
summary: A prominent cleric of Iṣfahán who fell gravely ill; quoted defending Mullá Ḥusayn against charges of heresy. Likely identical to the 'Ḥájí Kalbásí' entry above.
importance_reason: Recurring (three mentions) Iṣfahán cleric; flagged as a probable duplicate of 'Ḥájí Kalbásí'.

name: Ḥájí Muḥammad-Ismá‘íl-i-Dhabíḥ
summary: A devoted pilgrim surnamed Anís in the Lawḥ-i-Ra’ís (and known as Dhabíḥ), who reached Adrianople but had to depart to Gallipoli without beholding Bahá’u’lláh.
importance_reason: A believer honored with titles in Bahá’u’lláh's own writings, but with a single GPB mention and a brief story.

name: Ḥájí Muḥammad-Ja‘far-i-Ábadiyí
summary: A man invited (with Ḥájí Siyyid Asadu’lláh and others) to a meeting by the Mu‘tamid, who heeded counsel and refused the governor's invitation.
importance_reason: Minor named figure with a small recurring role (three mentions, single source).

name: Ḥájí Muḥammad-Karím Khán-i-Kirmání
summary: Qájár prince (1810–1871) who assumed leadership of the Shaykhí school after Siyyid Káẓim and became its most redoubtable line; an inordinately ambitious adversary of the Báb who wrote half a dozen anti-Bábí tracts and was reckoned by early Bábís as the Dajjál (Anti-Christ).
importance_reason: Foremost Shaykhí rival-claimant and prolific antagonist of the Báb; 22 mentions across both books.
characterizations: GPB: Powerful adversaries, among whom towered the figure of the inordinately ambitious and hypocritical Ḥájí Mírzá Karím Khán

Hurqalya Publications: He very early on rejected the messianic claims of the Bab and produced half a dozen or more anti-Babi-Baha'i tracts... The early Babis reportedly considered him to be the Anti-Christ or Dajjal.

name: Ḥájí Muḥammad-Riḍáy-i-Iṣfahání
summary: A seventy-year-old Bahá'í of ‘Ishqábád, martyred in 1889 when two ruffians stabbed him in thirty-two places in the open bazaar — a killing whose public trial brought official recognition of the Bahá'í community in Russian Turkistan.
importance_reason: Martyr of the historically significant ‘Ishqábád case (first official recognition of Bahá'ís); single mention but a taught event.

name: Ḥájí Muḥammad-Taqí
summary: A long-suffering believer of Nayríz, surnamed Ayyúb (Job) and mentioned by Bahá’u’lláh in the Súriy-i-Ayyúb, renowned for his former reputation for honesty and justice.
importance_reason: A believer named in Bahá’u’lláh's own writings (Súriy-i-Ayyúb); five mentions across both books.

name: Ḥájí Muḥammad-Taqíy-i-Kirmání
summary: A foremost merchant of Kirmán who embraced the Faith and was martyred, declaring his impatience to join his 'beloved Ḥusayn'.
importance_reason: Single-source (three mentions) Kirmán merchant-martyr with a memorable dying declaration.

name: Ḥájí Muḥammad-i-Karrádí
summary: A courageous man of the palm groves near old Baghdád who had led a hundred men against Ibráhím Páshá of Egypt, and who embraced the Faith of the Báb at seventy-five.
importance_reason: Single-mention believer with a colorful biographical anecdote.

name: Ḥájí Muḥammad-i-Tabrízí
summary: A well-known merchant of Mashhad (d. 1896) who was murdered and whose corpse was set on fire during persecutions.
importance_reason: Single-mention martyr known only by the manner of his death.

name: Ḥájí Muḥammad-Ḥusayn (father of Abá-Baṣír)
summary: A believer who raised the call to prayer with such fervor that an entire ‘Iráqí regiment refused to continue its shameful work; father of Abá-Baṣír.
importance_reason: Single-mention figure with one striking anecdote of the adhán halting a regiment.

name: Ḥájí Muḥammad-Ḥusayn-i-Kás̱hání
summary: An early-20th-century Covenant-breaker who represented Mírzá Muḥammad-‘Alí (the arch-breaker) in Egypt but failed utterly in his mission.
importance_reason: Single-mention Covenant-breaker agent; minor figure in the Covenant struggle.

name: Ḥájí Muḥammad-‘Alí, martyr of Zanján
summary: One of three Zanján believers (with Mírzá Riḍá and Ḥájí Muḥsin) condemned to have their veins slashed open.
importance_reason: Single-source (three mentions) martyr appearing as part of a martyr trio.

name: Ḥájí Muḥammad-‘Alíy-i-Lahárdí
summary: A man of the village of Lahárd who suffered greatly as a result of the murder of Mullá Taqí in Qazvín.
importance_reason: Single-mention villager tied to the aftermath of Mullá Taqí's murder.

name: Ḥájí Muḥammad-‘Alíy-i-Ḥamzih
summary: A pious divine of recognized exegetical skill who secretly arranged for the burial of mutilated martyr remains before prudently withdrawing into his house.
importance_reason: Single-mention cleric with a brief act of mercy; distinct given name from 'Ḥájí Mullá Muḥammad-i-Ḥamzih' (not merged).

name: Ḥájí Muḥsin, the surgeon, martyr of Zanján
summary: A surgeon of Zanján, one of three believers put to death after their arrival, alongside Mírzá Riḍá and Ḥájí Muḥammad-‘Alí.
importance_reason: Single-source (three mentions) martyr appearing as part of a martyr trio.

name: Ḥájí Muṣṭafá Khán
summary: An officer who, with ‘Abbás-Qulí Khán-i-Láríjání, swore on the Qur’án to treat the Ṭabarsí defenders as guests — a pledge soon betrayed.
importance_reason: Recurring (five mentions) participant in the treacherous Ṭabarsí truce; secondary figure, single source.

name: Ḥájí Muṣṭafá Khán-i-Turkamán
summary: A Mázindarání khán who counselled his sovereign and proposed enlisting his brother ‘Abdu’lláh Khán-i-Turkamán. Possibly distinct from 'Ḥájí Muṣṭafá Khán-i-Súr-Tíj' (different nisba; not merged).
importance_reason: Single-mention regional khán with a brief advisory role.

name: Ḥájí Muṣṭafá Ḵhán-i-Súr-Tíj
summary: A khán whose brother was ‘Abdu’lláh Khán; named in passing in the Dawn-Breakers. May be the same person as 'Ḥájí Muṣṭafá Khán-i-Turkamán' (both have a brother ‘Abdu’lláh Khán) — flagged.
importance_reason: Two bare mentions; flagged as a possible duplicate of 'Ḥájí Muṣṭafá Khán-i-Turkamán'.

name: Ḥájí Mu‘ínu’s-Salṭanih
summary: A Bábí chronicler whose narrative is cited in the Dawn-Breakers for details of the Báb's early business affairs and His pilgrimage.
importance_reason: A cited Bábí historian/source (four mentions) rather than an episode participant.

name: Ḥájí Mírzá Báqir
summary: A mujtahid of Tabríz to whose house the Báb was ignominiously led by order of the Grand Vizír, but who would not condescend to meet Him face to face.
importance_reason: Two mentions; a clerical figure tied to the events surrounding the Báb's last days in Tabríz.

name: Ḥájí Mírzá Jání
summary: A merchant of Káshán and early Bábí (surnamed Parpá) who hosted the Báb in his home for three nights during His passage through Káshán, and authored the earliest Bábí chronicle, the Nuqtatu’l-Kaf; martyred in 1852.
importance_reason: The earliest Bábí historian and the Báb's Káshán host; 23 mentions in the Dawn-Breakers.
characterizations: DB: when the Báb, the herald of the new Revelation, arrived in Káshán and for three nights lived as a guest in the house of Ḥájí Mírzá Jání.
Bahaipedia: Hájí Mírzá Jání Káshání... was a young merchant who accepted the Báb's message and authored the well-known early Babi chronicle, Nuqtatu'l-Kaf.

name: Ḥájí Mírzá Kamálu'd-Dín-i-Naráqí
summary: A Bábí of honorable rank and high culture at whose request Bahá’u’lláh revealed the Tablet of Kullu’ṭ-Ṭa‘ám during the Baghdád period — a request whose circumstances aggravated the rift with Mírzá Yaḥyá.
importance_reason: Occasioner of the revelation of the Lawḥ-i-Kullu’ṭ-Ṭa‘ám; four mentions across both books.
characterizations: GPB: The circumstances leading to the revelation of the Tablet of Kullu’ṭ-Ṭa‘ám, written during that period, at the request of Ḥájí Mírzá Kamálu’d-Dín-i-Naráqí, a Bábí of honorable rank and high culture

name: Ḥájí Mírzá Karím Ḵhán, son of Ibráhím Ḵhán-i-Qájár-i-Kirmání
common_name: Ḥájí Mírzá Karím Khán (son of Ibráhím Khán-i-Qájár)
summary: Son of Ibráhím Khán-i-Qájár-i-Kirmání, described as one-eyed and sparsely bearded; the father of the roster figure Ḥájí Muḥammad-Karím Khán-i-Kirmání. NOTE: not the Shaykhí leader himself — easily confused with him.
importance_reason: Single bare mention; a different person from the Shaykhí leader Ḥájí Muḥammad-Karím Khán (he is the leader's father) — flagged to prevent erroneous merge.

name: Ḥájí Mírzá Muḥammad-Taqí Afnán, the Vakílu’d-Dawlih
summary: A cousin of the Báb and leading merchant of Yazd (1830–1911) who dedicated his entire fortune to building the first Mashriqu’l-Adhkár (House of Worship) in ‘Ishqábád, personally supervising its construction; his dust reposes at the foot of Mt. Carmel near the Tomb of the Báb.
importance_reason: Builder of the first Bahá'í House of Worship and a member of the Afnán; a taught figure though only two GPB mentions.
characterizations: GPB: personally supervised by the venerable Ḥájí Mírzá Muḥammad-Taqí, the Vakílu’d-Dawlih, a cousin of the Báb, who dedicated his entire resources to its establishment, and whose dust now reposes at the foot of Mt. Carmel under the shadow of the Tomb of his beloved Kinsman

name: Ḥájí Mírzá Muḥammad-i-Ḵhurásání
summary: Leader of the Shaykhí community of Ṭihrán and an instructor there, who was approached by Mullá Ḥusayn but declined his invitation to accept the Message.
importance_reason: Two mentions; a Shaykhí leader who rejected the Cause, with a brief defined role.

name: Ḥájí Mírzá Músáy-i-Qumí
summary: A native of Qum who embraced the Faith and journeyed to Baghdád to meet Bahá’u’lláh; referenced as a correspondent of Bahá’u’lláh and as a guide in the search for Mírzá Aḥmad.
importance_reason: Recurring (three mentions) believer with a small named role including correspondence from Bahá’u’lláh.

name: Ḥájí Mírzá Náṣir
summary: The man said to have poisoned the Báb, and the same who had ordered Him to kiss the hand of Shaykh Abú-Turáb.
importance_reason: Single-mention figure but tied to a grave claim (the Báb's poisoning); minor antagonist.

name: Ḥájí Mírzá Riḍá-Qulí
summary: A half-brother of Bahá’u’lláh who was arrested and imprisoned for a month in the Síyáh-Chál; remained aloof from the Faith.
importance_reason: A half-brother of Bahá’u’lláh (Holy Family connection) though uncommitted; single mention.

name: Ḥájí Mírzá Siyyid Muḥammad
summary: The eldest maternal uncle of the Báb (1798–1876) and a member of the Afnán family; it was in answer to his questions about the prophecies of the Qá'im that Bahá’u’lláh revealed the Kitáb-i-Íqán (the Book of Certitude), upon reading which he became a believer.
importance_reason: The recipient for whom the Kitáb-i-Íqán was revealed — a foundational fact; two GPB mentions but doctrinally central.
characterizations: GPB: in reply to the questions addressed to Bahá'u'lláh by the as yet unconverted maternal uncle of the Báb, Ḥájí Mírzá Siyyid Muḥammad, while on a visit, with his brother, Ḥájí Mírzá Ḥasan-'Alí, to Karbilá
Bahaipedia: Hájí Mírzá Siyyid Muhammad (1798–1876), better known by the title Khál Akbar... Bahá'u'lláh revealed the Kitáb-i-Íqán in answer to some of his questions about his nephew's religion.

name: Ḥájí Mírzá Siyyid ‘Alí
aliases: Áqá Siyyid ‘Alí (maternal uncle of the Báb)
summary: The maternal uncle and guardian of the Báb (d. 1850) — the only father He had known since childhood and trusted guardian of His mother and wife; the first male relative to believe, and one of the Seven Martyrs of Ṭihrán, executed in February 1850.
importance_reason: The Báb's guardian-uncle and a Seven Martyrs of Ṭihrán hero; the most-mentioned (41) and highest-importance figure in this slice.
characterizations: GPB: the noble and serene Ḥájí Mírzá Siyyid 'Alí, the Báb's maternal uncle; the only father He had known since His childhood, His shield and support and the trusted guardian of both His mother and His wife

Bahaichronicles/Bahaipedia: known to Iranian Bahá'ís as 'Khál-i A'zam,' the Greater Maternal Uncle... became a Bábí in 1845 through Quddús... the only male member of the Báb's family to convert in his lifetime, martyred among the Seven Martyrs in February 1850.

name: Ḥájí Mírzá Siyyid ‘Alíy-i-Tafrís̱hí, the Majdu’l-As̱hraf
summary: A Bahá'í of Tafrísh (the Majdu’l-Ashraf), father-in-law of Mírzá Ḥasan-i-Vazír; named in passing in both books.
importance_reason: Two mentions; a notable Bahá'í identified mainly by family ties and his title.
